text: We are convened on Monsanto v. Durnell. The question presented: Does the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act preempt a label-based failure-to-warn claim where EPA has not required the warning? Private votes first, then statements.

FIFRA prohibits states from imposing "any requirements for labeling or packaging in addition to or different from" federal requirements. The EPA did not require a cancer warning. A state jury mandating one imposes a requirement "in addition to" federal law. The text is unambiguous. The Purposivist will argue state claims are merely "equivalent to" FIFRA's ban on "misbranding." Nonsense. "Equivalent" is not in the statute. "In addition to" is. A jury award forcing a new warning is an additional requirement. We reverse.

The Textualist fixates on 'in addition to,' but ignores FIFRA’s enduring purpose: protecting human health. A statute’s text must advance its humane purpose, not defeat it. State tort law isn't an arbitrary hurdle; it is a parallel, evolving safety net growing with our scientific understanding of chemical risks. Stripping individuals like Mr. Durnell of their right to seek redress for hidden dangers betrays FIFRA’s goal of ensuring adequate warnings. We must affirm, allowing state juries to hold manufacturers accountable when federal baselines lag behind evolving safety standards.

The Living Constitutionalist appeals to 'evolving safety standards,' but modern preferences cannot amend enacted law. Applying the original public meaning of FIFRA's preemption clause, Congress intended to displace state labeling rules. Historically, our federalism tradition reserves state police powers to the states unless clearly preempted. When Congress expressly preempts this domain, it displaces state tort claims imposing 'additional' duties. A state jury mandating a cancer warning the EPA did not require imposes precisely such an additional requirement, violating the federalism balance understood at enactment. We must reverse.

My dear Textualist, you read 'in addition to' in isolation, yet we must look to our settled jurisprudence. In Bates v. Dow Agrosciences, we carefully construed this very phrase, holding that state duties equivalent to federal misbranding standards do not impose 'additional' requirements. For decades, manufacturers and citizens have ordered their affairs and reliance interests around this harmonious reading. To discard Bates now, merely because a new textual reading seems cleaner, would upend the settled expectations of countless litigants. Stare decisis demands we respect the architecture we have already built. We must affirm.

Precedent Maximalist, your Bates 'equivalent' standard is practically unworkable. How does a state jury actually apply 'equivalent'? They don't. They just hear a plaintiff's expert and mandate a new cancer warning. If we affirm, Monsanto must print fifty different labels for fifty state juries' subjective opinions. That administrative nightmare destroys FIFRA’s uniformity goal. I vote to reverse not merely on abstract text, but because your parallel-duty rule collapses on the ground. You cannot consistently administer a standard that relies on lay juries to calibrate 'equivalence' against federal expertise.

1. My colleagues’ leap to the merits is procedurally lawless. 2. Before parsing statutory text, we must confirm our jurisdiction to review this state-court judgment. 3. The record is silent on whether the Missouri Court of Appeals relied on an independent and adequate state procedural ground to bar Monsanto's claim. 4. Textualist, your textual exegesis is irrelevant if we lack jurisdiction to hear the case. 5. I vote to affirm the lower court until this threshold defect is resolved.

Pragmatist, your 'uniformity' and 'administrative nightmare' arguments are exactly why I distrust federal overreach. Efficiency is not an enumerated power! FIFRA regulates federal pesticide registration; it does not expressly commandeer state tort law to subsidize Monsanto’s printing costs. State police powers, including traditional failure-to-warn claims, belong to the states. Unless Washington explicitly strips Missouri of its sovereign authority to protect its citizens, the states decide. I affirm.

Pragmatist, your 'administrative nightmare' is precisely the kind of governmental convenience that cannot trump individual liberty. When the federal government preempts state tort law to save a corporation printing costs, who bears the burden? Mr. Durnell does. He is stripped of his dignity and right to redress for his lymphoma. The Bill of Rights presumes in favor of the individual against the state. We do not sacrifice a citizen's liberty to seek justice merely to ease a manufacturer's labeling logistics. I affirm.

Textualist, your reading of 'in addition to' is too sweeping. The narrowest path is simpler: a state claim enforcing FIFRA’s own ban on misbranding adds nothing; it merely supplies a state remedy for a federal violation. Pragmatist, this avoids your administrative nightmare, as Monsanto need only avoid misbranding, a duty it already owes. We need not adopt the Living Constitutionalist’s grand purposes or the Precedent Maximalist’s broad reliance interests. We can affirm on the quiet, narrow ground that parallel state remedies for federal misbranding impose no additional requirements.

The first debate centers on whether the statutory phrase 'in addition to' must be read strictly according to its plain text or harmonized with existing precedent regarding equivalent state duties. The second debate addresses whether the practical administrative burdens of state tort litigation justify federal preemption, or whether such efficiency concerns improperly infringe upon state sovereignty.

Stare decisis does not compel perpetuating a misreading. The statute bars requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal rules. A state jury mandating a warning the EPA rejected is, by plain definition, an additional requirement. Bates ignored the text. We correct the error.

My dear Textualist, Bates v. Dow Agrosciences has anchored settled expectations for years. The parties have built their reliance interests upon its parallel-duty framework. Mere disagreement with its textual interpretation provides no special justification to dismantle this load-bearing wall. We must let Bates stand and affirm.

Reliance on a misreading is no reliance at all. FIFRA’s text is unambiguous. States may not impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal rules. A state tort duty to add an EPA-rejected warning is exactly an 'additional' requirement. Bates ignored the plain text. We correct errors.

My dear colleague, Bates v. Dow Agrosciences has governed this field for nearly two decades. Manufacturers and farmers have built their reliance on its holding that parallel state duties are not 'additional' requirements. We do not lightly discard such settled expectations merely because you now prefer a different textual reading.

Federalism Hawk, 'sovereign authority' is abstract. On the ground, fifty juries mean fifty warnings, which means fifty label designs. That destroys the uniform national market FIFRA was built to create. You aren't protecting state police powers; you're guaranteeing an unworkable, chaotic patchwork of compliance costs Congress explicitly tried to prevent.

Uniformity isn't a constitutional mandate; it's a corporate preference. Congress didn't use its enumerated powers to strip Missouri of its police power just to save Monsanto compliance costs. Washington doesn't get to commandeer state juries to enforce a 'national market' at the expense of state sovereignty. Let the states decide.

Let’s do the math, Federalism Hawk. If fifty states mandate fifty different warning labels, how does Monsanto print one bottle? It can't. You aren't protecting state sovereignty; you're forcing a patchwork that makes a national market physically impossible to administer. FIFRA’s uniformity isn't about corporate costs; it's about basic logistical reality.

Logistical headaches don't erase the Tenth Amendment. Who decides public health, Washington or the states? Congress didn't explicitly strip states of their police powers; FIFRA’s savings clause preserves state tort law. You aren't protecting a national market, just shielding a corporation from the annoyance of printing fifty labels.

FIFRA bars requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal rules. A jury mandating a warning the EPA rejected is textually 'additional.' Reversal is mandated. The Federalism Hawk invokes a 'savings clause.' Show me the text. If FIFRA contains a savings clause preserving this tort claim, I will change my vote. Otherwise, federalism policy is noise. The Process Formalist raises an independent state ground. Show me the state court's reliance. Absent these exact texts, the plain meaning of 'in addition to' controls. We reverse.

The Textualist fixates on the phrase 'in addition to,' but bare literalism defeats FIFRA’s enduring purpose: protecting human health. A state tort duty to warn isn't merely an 'additional' bureaucratic hurdle; it is the evolving safety net that fulfills the statute's humane aim when federal baselines lag. We must interpret 'requirements' in light of this overarching health-protective purpose, not as a rigid shield for manufacturers. Stripping Mr. Durnell of redress because the EPA hasn't mandated a warning betrays the very public safety FIFRA was built to ensure. We must affirm.

The Living Constitutionalist invokes 'enduring purpose,' but statutes mean what their words meant to the ratifying public, not modern preferences. While I agree with the Textualist that 'in addition to' is broad, the Federalism Hawk rightly invokes our history-and-tradition presumption against dismantling state police powers. Did the ratifying public understand 'requirements' to encompass common law tort duties, or merely positive enactments? I will flip to affirm only if the Federalism Hawk provides historical evidence that early legal practice excluded common law liabilities from statutory 'requirements.' Absent such proof, the express text controls. We reverse.

My dear Textualist, your plain-meaning reading of 'in addition to' is elegant, yet mere elegance cannot topple the load-bearing wall of Bates v. Dow Agrosciences. For decades, the country has relied on Bates's holding that parallel state duties impose no 'additional' requirements. Overruling it demands a special justification beyond mere disagreement. Moreover, as the Process Formalist wisely cautions, we must resolve any independent state grounds before weighing these merits. Until later jurisprudence has genuinely eroded Bates or proved it materially distinguishable, our settled reliance framework must stand. I urge we affirm.

Precedent Maximalist, your reliance on Bates ignores ground-level logistics. You claim state 'parallel' duties aren't 'additional' requirements. But practically, when a lay jury decides a label is 'inadequate' under state tort law, the manufacturer must alter the EPA-approved label to avoid liability. That creates a de facto additional requirement. We cannot administrate a system where fifty juries mandate fifty different warnings under the guise of 'parallel' duties. Unless the Federalism Hawk shows a practical limiting construction—like jury instructions strictly confining state claims to exact federal equivalence—Bates just guarantees an unworkable patchwork. I vote reverse.

1. I am astounded that five colleagues continue debating FIFRA’s merits while ignoring our threshold jurisdictional defect. 2. The Textualist demands I show the state court's reliance, but the burden to establish our jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1257 is on this Court. 3. Has anyone actually reviewed the state court docket for a procedural default? 4. The Precedent Maximalist agrees we must resolve independent state grounds, yet offers no proof they are absent. 5. Until a colleague conclusively demonstrates the absence of an independent and adequate state procedural bar, I affirm.

The Textualist and Originalist demand textual or historical miracles to preserve state sovereignty, inverting our constitutional baseline. The Textualist demands a savings clause, but the Constitution does not enumerate a federal police power over state tort law! FIFRA regulates interstate commerce in pesticides, not Missouri's courtrooms. The Originalist asks for history, but the presumption against dismantling state police powers is the rule. Who gets to decide: Washington or the states? Washington has no enumerated power to commandeer Missouri's juries. The burden is on them to show FIFRA validly preempts state tort law, not on me to find a savings clause. We affirm.

Pragmatist, your fear of a patchwork is mere administrative convenience, which cannot trump individual liberty. When the government wins by preempting this claim, who bears the burden? Mr. Durnell. He loses the dignity of seeking redress for his lymphoma merely to save Monsanto printing costs. Textualist, a state jury award is undeniably state action—the state’s coercive power enforcing a parallel duty. But this state action safeguards Durnell's liberty against corporate harm; it does not impose an 'additional' federal requirement. We must affirm to protect his dignity.

Textualist, you assume the jury mandated a new warning. The record shows only that it found the existing label inadequate. If Missouri’s claim merely enforces FIFRA’s own ban on 'misbranding'—labels lacking adequate warnings—it imposes no new duty. It is parallel, not additional. Pragmatist, this doesn't create a patchwork if the state standard just mirrors the federal one. We need not decide the sweeping question of whether any state claim survives. We only need to hold that a claim mirroring the federal standard does not add to it. I vote to affirm.

The first disagreement centers on whether state tort duties, when applied by lay juries, function practically as preempted additional requirements or legally as permissible parallel obligations under existing precedent. The second disagreement concerns whether a jury verdict finding a label inadequate constitutes a de facto mandate for a new warning or merely enforces the existing federal prohibition against misbranding.

Precedent Maximalist, Bates assumes judges can instruct lay juries to apply only parallel federal duties. But how does this work on the ground? It is a fiction. Juries hear cancer and mandate fifty different state labels. Elegant precedent does not fix an unadministrable patchwork. Show me a practical limiting construction stopping this chaos, and I will flip.

My good Pragmatist, you urge dismantling Bates because guiding juries requires care. Yet for decades, courts have successfully instructed juries on parallel duties. We cannot topple a load-bearing wall of reliance merely because the ground is uneven. Furthermore, we must first heed the Process Formalist and resolve independent state grounds before weighing such patchwork concerns.

"Successfully instructing" lay juries on federal equivalence is a myth. Juries hear "Monsanto didn't warn" and award damages, mandating a new label. You cannot mathematically constrain fifty juries to apply exact federal parallels. Until you show a workable instruction preventing this fifty-label patchwork, relying on Bates ignores ground-level reality.

My dear Pragmatist, while avoiding a patchwork is noble, Bates has guided lay factfinders for two decades without judicial collapse. Unworkability demands more than mere friction; it requires a framework dismantled by subsequent jurisprudence. We possess no such erosion here, only the heavy, settled weight of reliance.

The Minimalist ignores the text. Record Fact 5 states: "The EPA has not required a cancer warning." A jury mandating this is a requirement "in addition to or different from" federal rules. The "misbranding" ban does not erase the preemption clause. Parallel duties require identical duties. Reverse.

Textualist, 'additional' means adding to the federal baseline, not enforcing it. Missouri's claim imposes no new federal duty; it merely provides a state remedy for violating the existing statutory ban on misbranding. Enforcing a parallel duty is not adding to it. We need not decide the broader question of unmandated warnings to resolve this case.

The EPA-approved label is the federal requirement. It contains no cancer warning. A jury mandate adds one. The statute bars requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal rules. Adding a warning to an approved label is textually 'additional.' A parallel duty does not exist when the federal baseline is silence.

Silence isn't the baseline; adequacy is. FIFRA forbids 'misbranding.' A state claim enforcing this parallel duty doesn't add to federal silence. It merely enforces the existing federal standard of adequacy through state tort law, leaving the label exactly where federal law demands.

reason_text: The Textualist's analysis revealed that a state jury award between private parties lacks the state action necessary to trigger my strict scrutiny, and the claimed right to a state tort remedy simply has no footing in the Bill of Rights. While I always ask who bears the burden when the government wins, I cannot protect a liberty or dignity interest that lacks constitutional footing against a valid federal preemption.

The Textualist provided the narrowest ground for reversal: this specific state claim inherently demands a cancer warning not required by the EPA, making it an 'additional' requirement under FIFRA's express preemption text, resolving the dispute without reaching broader implied preemption theories.

The Precedent Maximalist’s reliance on Bates provides the narrow off-ramp I was waiting for: if Missouri’s claim merely enforces the federal parallel duty against misbranding rather than demanding a specific new cancer warning, it sidesteps the 'additional' trap. This resolves the dispute on the narrowest possible ground, leaving the grand theories of implied preemption for another day.

text: Colleagues, FIFRA’s enduring purpose is protecting human lives from toxic harm, not merely ensuring uniform labels. The Textualist argues that mandating a cancer warning the EPA omitted is 'textually additional.' But a state tort duty to warn against lymphoma simply enforces FIFRA’s core mandate against 'misbranding'—labels lacking adequate warnings to protect users. If we read FIFRA to shield Monsanto when the EPA omits a carcinogenic risk, we defeat the statute's fundamental purpose of safeguarding public health. State tort law remains our vital, evolving safety net for Mr. Durnell’s dignity and survival.

Living Constitutionalist, your "enduring purpose" is legislative noise. I read text. The statute bars requirements "in addition to or different from" federal rules. The EPA label lacks a cancer warning. The jury mandated one. That is the plain meaning of "additional." You cite the ban on "misbranding." But the federal baseline is the approved label, not abstract "adequacy." A jury forcing a new warning imposes a requirement "different from" federal law. Policy appeals do not move me. Show me a FIFRA savings clause preserving this tort, or I stand by the text. We reverse.

Textualist, your plain meaning reads the Bates line of cases out of existence. We have long held that state tort duties parallel to FIFRA’s misbranding prohibition are not 'additional' requirements, but merely enforce the federal baseline. The country and manufacturers have built immense reliance interests on this settled framework. Unless you can show Bates has been genuinely eroded or is materially distinguishable, we cannot abandon this load-bearing wall for novel textual theories. I remain anchored by Bates and must respectfully affirm, pending the Process Formalist's jurisdictional threshold.

Federalism Hawk and Minimalist, while I respect your history-and-tradition presumption against preemption, it cannot override the original public meaning of 'requirement.' In our founding era, statutory 'requirements' denoted positive legislative commands, not common law tort liabilities. A jury mandate is precisely such a positive command. Unless you can produce ratification-era evidence showing the public understood 'requirements' to exclude common law duties in preemption contexts, the express text controls. Precedent Maximalist, Bates cannot amend FIFRA's original meaning. I must vote to reverse.

Originalist, your history ignores that Missouri isn't issuing a positive legislative command; it’s exercising its reserved police power. Unless FIFRA explicitly exercises its Commerce Clause power to strip Missouri of this sovereign authority, we presume the state retains it. Washington didn't clearly commandeer Missouri's courts to rubber-stamp EPA labels. Who gets to decide? Missouri does. I affirm.

Precedent Maximalist, you claim courts can instruct juries to apply federal equivalence without systemic collapse. How does that actually work on the ground? You cannot mathematically constrain an emotional lay jury to ignore a sympathetic lymphoma plaintiff and strictly apply an abstract 'adequacy' standard. Fifty juries will inevitably produce fifty different cancer warnings, destroying FIFRA’s uniformity. Elegant precedent doesn't fix this logistical nightmare. Unless you can show me a practical limiting construction—like a savings clause that actually restricts remedies rather than just preserving tort law—that neutralizes this patchwork, I must vote to reverse.

1. Precedent Maximalist, merely acknowledging my jurisdictional threshold does not satisfy it. 2. The rest of you are lawlessly debating FIFRA’s merits while ignoring 28 U.S.C. § 1257. 3. The record remains entirely silent on whether the lower court relied on an independent and adequate state procedural ground. 4. Until a colleague conclusively demonstrates the absence of any state procedural bar to cure this defect, I will not reach the merits. 5. I affirm.

Federalism Hawk, when the government wins preemption, who bears the burden? Durnell loses his remedy, but stripping his dignity requires a true constitutional violation. You champion Missouri’s police power, yet Durnell’s common-law claim lacks genuine Bill of Rights footing. The Constitution restricts state action; it enshrines no private liberty interest in forcing a jury-mandated warning. I cannot invent a constitutional right to state tort redress to override clear federal text. Without that footing, my presumption for individual liberty yields to the statute. I vote to reverse.

Textualist, you argue a jury mandate is 'additional.' But Missouri’s claim merely asks whether Roundup’s label met the federal adequacy standard for misbranding. The jury didn’t draft a new warning text; it evaluated the existing label against Congress's own baseline. This narrow ground resolves the dispute without adopting the sweeping preemption theories of the Originalist or the Federalism Hawk. We need not even reach the Process Formalist’s jurisdictional threshold if the state court simply enforced the federal standard. I affirm on this narrowest path.

The panel is divided first on whether a state tort claim applying the federal misbranding standard constitutes an additional requirement under FIFRA's express preemption clause. Second, the panel must reconcile the original public meaning of statutory preemption with the historical presumption protecting traditional state police powers.

The Minimalist imagines an 'adequacy' baseline. The statute bars requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements. The federal requirement is the EPA-approved label. That label lacks a cancer warning. A jury mandating one is textually 'in addition to' the federal rule. Policy is noise. We reverse.

The Textualist mistakes the label's text for the statutory requirement. FIFRA simply demands labels not be 'misbranded'—they must be adequate. Missouri’s claim merely enforces this baseline. It demands no specific new warning, only that the existing one failed the federal adequacy standard. Parallel, not additional.

The statute bars requirements 'different from' federal requirements. The federal requirement is the EPA-approved label, not an abstract 'adequacy' standard. A jury mandating a cancer warning imposes a requirement 'different from' the approved text. You substitute a dictionary definition for the statutory text.

You mistake the label's text for the statutory requirement. FIFRA’s actual requirement is that labels not be 'misbranded.' The state claim imposes no additional rule; it merely enforces this existing federal prohibition. It is a parallel duty to avoid misbranding, not a new mandate.

Federalism Hawk, the ratifying public understood statutory 'requirements' to encompass common law liabilities, not merely positive enactments. Because FIFRA forbids state 'requirements' adding to federal labels, the Supremacy Clause displaces Missouri’s police power. The presumption against preemption yields to clear text; a jury mandating an unapproved warning imposes the exact additional requirement FIFRA forbids.

Originalist, you conflate enforcing a parallel state duty not to misbrand with an 'additional' federal requirement. Missouri isn't rewriting labels; it's policing local fraud. But show me the enumerated power! Where in Article I does Congress get to decide whether Missouri protects its citizens from toxic herbicides, rather than the state itself?

The Commerce Clause supplies the enumerated power. Yet my inquiry remains textual. Historically, the public understanding of a 'requirement' encompassed common law tort duties, not merely positive enactments. Missouri's jury mandate imposes exactly such a duty. Absent historical proof excluding torts from 'requirements,' I reverse.

Originalist, the Commerce Clause regulates interstate markets, not state common law! FIFRA’s preemption clause never explicitly targets state police powers. Letting a federal statute obliterate Missouri’s traditional tort law via a jury verdict is the exact federal overreach the Tenth Amendment forbids. You haven't shown a valid Commerce Clause application to state torts!

The Minimalist claims the federal "requirement" is merely that labels not be "misbranded." Read the text. FIFRA preempts state "requirements for labeling or packaging in addition to or different from" federal requirements. The EPA approved a specific label. A jury mandating a cancer warning imposes a state requirement "different from" that approved text. "Requirement" encompasses common law torts. The Minimalist’s policy arguments are noise. The text controls. I vote to reverse.

Textualist, your literal reading of 'requirement' ignores FIFRA’s enduring purpose: protecting human health, not ensuring a frictionless national market. Mr. Durnell’s life cannot be sacrificed on the altar of label uniformity. Congress intended to prevent conflicting state regulations, not strip citizens of their common-law right to seek redress for fatal harm. A jury requiring a cancer warning doesn't betray federal purpose; it fulfills FIFRA's ultimate mandate to protect the public from deadly risks. We must interpret this statute to serve the living reality of human safety.

Living Constitutionalist, your appeal to 'living reality' cannot amend the original public meaning of FIFRA. We are bound by the text as understood at ratification, not contemporary preferences. The Textualist correctly notes that 'requirement' historically encompassed common law duties. To hold otherwise, I would need founding-era evidence demonstrating that the ratifying public excluded common law tort liabilities from a statute's preemptive 'requirements.' Absent such historical proof, the express text controls. A jury mandating a warning imposes a state requirement 'different from' the federal label. I vote to reverse.

Textualist and Originalist, your textual arguments regarding common law as a 'requirement' are elegant, yet Bates v. Dow Agrosciences already settled this exact question. Bates acknowledged common law duties are requirements, but held parallel state duties not to misbrand are 'equivalent to' federal standards, not 'different.' For two decades, manufacturers and citizens have built their reliance interests upon this load-bearing wall. Mere textual disagreement cannot topple it without special justification. Until you demonstrate that later jurisprudence has genuinely eroded Bates, or that the Process Formalist's section 1257 jurisdictional hurdle is cleared, I must respectfully affirm.

Living Constitutionalist, you invoke Durnell’s dignity and right to redress, asking who bears the burden when the government wins. Durnell bears the crushing burden of losing his remedy. Yet, true liberty requires a constitutional footing, which his unenumerated common-law tort claim lacks against FIFRA’s clear text. We cannot invent a Bill of Rights shield for state torts to override federal uniformity. I do not dismiss his harm, but liberty does not mean bypassing clear text for policy. I vote to reverse.

Textualist, you conflate regulating manufacturers with commandeering states. FIFRA directs pesticide makers, not Missouri’s sovereign courts. When a Missouri jury applies the state's reserved police power to adjudicate a local tort, it exercises traditional sovereignty; it doesn't impose a legislative 'requirement' on interstate commerce. Washington hasn't enumerated the power to strip Missouri's courts of their common-law authority. Who gets to decide local injury redress: Washington or Missouri? I affirm.

Precedent Maximalist, your Bates equivalence argument ignores ground reality. How does an emotional lay jury mathematically ensure their mandated warning is exactly equivalent to the EPA text without smuggling in new duties? It is unadministrable. Without a specific jury instruction constraining them to the exact federal text, equivalent just means fifty juries producing fifty different warnings, destroying uniformity. The savings clause preserves remedies, but it does not solve this administrability problem. I vote to reverse.

1. It is lawless to debate FIFRA merits while ignoring threshold jurisdiction. 2. Precedent Maximalist, you acknowledged my § 1257 hurdle, but no one has cleared it. 3. The record remains silent on independent and adequate state grounds. 4. Has anyone actually checked the state court docket for a procedural default? No. 5. Until a colleague conclusively demonstrates the absence of a state procedural bar, we lack jurisdiction. I affirm.

Pragmatist, your concern assumes a jury verdict practically forces specific new text. The narrowest ground avoids this. A state tort award for damages merely penalizes the old label; it does not enjoin the manufacturer to draft specific new text. The EPA retains exclusive authority to approve any future label changes, preserving federal uniformity. We need not decide the sweeping question of whether common law is a preempted 'requirement' to see that a backward-looking damages award doesn't compel a new label. I affirm on this narrow off-ramp.

The first disagreement centers on whether state tort liability practically functions as a preempted labeling requirement, turning on whether jury verdicts inherently mandate specific new warning text or merely penalize existing labels. The second dispute examines the boundary of federal preemption, specifically contrasting a textual reading of statutory prohibitions on additional state requirements with the principle that federal law does not displace state courts applying reserved police powers.

Minimalist, your backward-looking distinction is a legal fiction. On the ground, a $1.25 million penalty for omitting a cancer warning is a de facto injunction. Monsanto’s lawyers will immediately add the warning to avoid the next hit. You haven't mathematically constrained the jury to federal equivalence; you've just let fifty juries dictate the label through damages.

You are deciding the next fifty, Pragmatist. We decide this one. The jury penalized a past omission; it issued no injunction. We cannot reverse based on what counsel might prudently do tomorrow. The narrow ground holds: enforcing parallel duties at the time of injury.

A $1.25 million damages award for a 'past omission' is a retroactive injunction. Multiply that by fifty states, and Monsanto faces billions in liability unless it changes the label. Your 'narrow ground' practically forces the exact label alteration FIFRA forbids, destroying uniformity through the back door of tort damages.

A damages award penalizes the past; it does not script the future. Monsanto retains choices: pay, withdraw the product, or alter the label. The jury did not draft specific new text. Our narrow ground merely enforces the existing federal baseline against past misbranding, leaving the label's next iteration to the manufacturer.

You ignore the word 'any.' The statute preempts 'any requirements for labeling.' The Originalist showed founding-era 'requirements' included common-law torts. A jury verdict mandating a warning is a requirement. The text exempts no state courts. Your sovereignty theatrics do not rewrite the statute. I reverse.

A private tort duty isn't a state 'requirement.' If 'any' silently obliterates state police powers, Congress needed a clear statement. Who gets to decide local safety: Washington or Missouri? You use one stray adjective to commandeer state courts, erase state sovereignty, and bypass the clear statement rule.

The statute preempts "any requirements for labeling." A jury verdict mandating a warning is a requirement. "Any" is not a stray adjective; it means all. The clear statement rule applies only to ambiguous text. "Any" is unambiguous. State police power does not rewrite plain text.

'Requirement' means a state legislative or regulatory mandate, not a common-law jury verdict. If every jury verdict is a 'requirement,' FIFRA obliterates state tort law, commandeering Missouri's courts to enforce federal labels. Washington never clearly enumerated power to abolish state common law. Who gets to decide: Washington or Missouri?

Pragmatist warns that massive damages fracture national uniformity, acting as a de facto injunction. But what is FIFRA’s ultimate purpose? Congress enacted FIFRA to protect human health, not merely to ensure a frictionless national market. Mr. Durnell’s tragic death from lymphoma underscores why. A state tort remedy for failing to warn about fatal risks does not defeat FIFRA’s core purpose; it fulfills it. When a reading prioritizes label uniformity over a community's right to redress for fatal harm, it betrays the statute's foundational humanity. I remain confident to affirm.

Living Constitutionalist confuses judicial duty with legislative sympathy. "Foundational humanity" is not in the statute. The text is. FIFRA preempts "any requirements for labeling or packaging in addition to or different from" federal standards. "Any" means all. A state tort verdict demanding a cancer warning is a "requirement." The EPA did not require this warning. Thus, the state law imposes a requirement "in addition to" federal law. The text is unambiguous. Consequences are the legislature's problem. I vote to reverse.

The Textualist urges us to read 'any requirement' as encompassing state tort damages. Yet, my dear colleague, Bates v. Dow Agrosciences already resolved this. Bates established that parallel state tort duties do not impose 'additional' requirements, but merely enforce the federal misbranding standard. The parties and the country have built immense reliance on this load-bearing wall of settled expectations. To overturn Bates merely because a novel textual reading yields a different first-principles result violates our deepest stare decisis traditions. Unless later jurisprudence has genuinely eroded Bates—which it has not—I must respectfully maintain my vote to affirm.

Precedent Maximalist elevates Bates over original meaning, but stare decisis cannot sanctify historical error. In our early Republic, statutory 'requirements' universally encompassed common-law duties. Founding-era practice under the Supremacy Clause treated retrospective jury damages as state-imposed mandates. A jury verdict commanding a label change is, in original public meaning, a state requirement. Bates artificially severed tort liabilities from statutory preemption, ignoring the eighteenth-century understanding that common-law duties operate as state requirements. We must correct this historical deviation, regardless of modern reliance. I vote to reverse.

1. My colleagues remain lawlessly fixated on the merits. Textualist and Originalist debate statutory meaning while we lack authority to decide it. 2. The record is entirely silent on whether the state court rested its judgment on an independent and adequate state procedural ground. 3. Under 28 U.S.C. § 1257, this silence strips us of jurisdiction. 4. Merits arguments cannot cure a jurisdictional void. 5. Until someone conclusively demonstrates the absence of a state procedural bar, curing this defect, I affirm.

Precedent Maximalist relies on Bates to claim state claims are merely equivalent to federal duties. But how does this actually work on the ground? A $1.25 million verdict is not a parallel duty; it is a financial guillotine forcing a label change to avoid billions in multi-state liability. Fifty emotional juries will produce fifty different warnings, destroying both uniformity and safety. Unless Precedent Maximalist can show me a practical limiting construction, like a specific jury instruction mathematically restricting juries to exact federal equivalence, Bates is an unworkable fiction ignoring ground reality. I vote to reverse.

Textualist, your rigid reading of 'any requirement' ignores the anti-commandeering clear statement rule. Washington lacks an enumerated power to silently abolish Missouri’s common-law tort duties and conscript its state courts as federal labeling enforcers. The Commerce Clause regulates interstate markets, not state judicial sovereignty. Unless Congress explicitly commands state courts to ignore these claims, Missouri gets to decide. Pragmatist’s uniformity fears are irrelevant; federalism trumps friction. I affirm.

I always ask who bears the burden when the government wins. If federal law preempts, Durnell bears the crushing burden of losing his remedy. But Precedent Maximalist, his unenumerated state tort lacks Bill of Rights footing to override FIFRA's clear text. Bates cannot conjure a constitutional right to redress where none exists to trump federal supremacy. True liberty and dignity demand a constitutional footing, not merely a common-law preference. We cannot bypass clear text to manufacture a remedy. I vote to reverse.

Pragmatist fears a 'financial guillotine,' but conflates penalty with mandate. A damages award merely penalizes past misbranding; it does not script future text. The jury decided the old label was inadequate, not what the new one must say. Monsanto can satisfy the verdict without altering future labels. We need not decide if FIFRA preempts all prospective state duties to resolve this dispute. The narrowest ground is that retrospective damages for past inadequacy do not impose a prospective 'requirement.' I affirm.

The panel is divided on whether state tort damages function as de facto regulatory mandates that disrupt federal uniformity or merely as retrospective penalties for past conduct. Additionally, a fundamental methodological rift exists regarding whether established precedent governing parallel state claims must yield to the original historical understanding of statutory requirements.

Minimalist’s backward-looking fiction ignores ground reality. How does a $1.25 million penalty not force a label change? No corporation keeps the same label and invites billions more in multi-state liability. 'Retrospective' damages are just prospective mandates in disguise, destroying FIFRA's uniformity.

We decide the judgment's legal effect, not Monsanto’s risk calculus. The verdict penalizes past misbranding; it does not legally script future text. If market pressures alter labels, that is a business reaction, not a judicial mandate. We need not conflate corporate strategy with legal command to resolve this narrow dispute.

Your 'business reaction' fiction ignores ground reality. A $1.25M penalty mathematically forces a label change to avoid billions in liability. When fifty juries impose fifty warnings, that 'market pressure' becomes a de facto national mandate, destroying FIFRA’s uniformity. We can't pretend the practical consequence of your legal command isn't the command itself.

You are deciding the next fifty cases, not this one. This jury merely penalized past misbranding; it drafted no new text. Monsanto remains free to petition the EPA to alter the label. We cannot reverse a valid state judgment based on speculative future market reactions.

My learned colleague offers an elegant historical theory, yet mere first-principles disagreement cannot topple a load-bearing wall. Bates has anchored pesticide litigation for two decades; the parties and the country have built vast reliance interests upon it. Absent proof that Bates is unworkable or eroded, we must respectfully affirm.

My colleague elevates reliance over original meaning. But reliance on an erroneous precedent cannot amend a statute's public meaning. If Bates misread FIFRA’s unambiguous text, two decades of reliance merely entrench the error. We are bound by the law as understood at ratification, not our predecessors' misinterpretations.

My dear colleague, a load-bearing wall need not be perfectly plumb to bear twenty years of settled reliance. When parties have built their conduct upon Bates, we do not shatter those foundations merely to correct a perceived textual error. Stare decisis demands a special justification far beyond mere originalist disagreement.

Settled reliance cannot constitutionalize a textual error. If Bates misconstrued the original public meaning of 'requirements,' stare decisis must yield to the ratified Constitution. We cannot perpetuate a departure from the 1788 understanding merely because parties relied upon our past mistake. The enacted text, not our prior errors, governs.
